3 min read
Just now

--

You tap your phone. Something happens.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

But the glass doesn’t move. There’s no button. No mechanical click. No pressure switch underneath.
So what is actually going on?
The answer is electricity. Specifically, an invisible electric field sitting just above the surface of the screen, waiting for your finger to mess with it.
Non member? Click here.
It’s not really “just glass”
Start here. The screen is not a plain sheet of glass.
Underneath that glossy surface is a layer of a material called Indium Tin Oxide, or ITO. It’s a conductor, but it’s also completely transparent. That’s the key trick. You can coat the entire screen with it and nobody sees it.
This ITO layer is connected to a controller chip. The chip runs a tiny electrical current through the layer constantly. The result is a uniform electric field across the whole screen surface.
Your phone is basically running a low-power electric grid on its face, 24/7, invisible to you.
Your finger is a conductor
